sections.about: This metric measures renewable fuel consumption under BRSR Principle 6, Essential Indicator 1. In steel, renewable fuels may include biofuels or other non-fossil substitutes used in heating, preheating or auxiliary processes. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata, SAIL and Jindal all reported zero renewable fuel in FY2023. JSW increased from zero to 169,293 GJ by FY2025, while Jindal Stainless rose from zero in FY2023 to 245,769 GJ in FY2025, becoming the leader. Jindal’s FY2025 BRSR explicitly cites biofuel replacing LSHS in preheating and WBF, alongside fuel-saving and thermal energy initiatives. SAIL reports zero renewable fuel across all three years, and Tata also reports zero in all years, suggesting both companies’ renewable strategies are currently electricity-capacity led rather than fuel-substitution led.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 3rd, tied with SAIL at zero, and is 100% below Jindal Stainless, the best performer. All four peers were tied at zero in FY2023; Tata stayed at zero through FY2024 and FY2025 while JSW and Jindal began reporting renewable fuel consumption, resulting in Tata slipping to rank 3 from FY2024 onwards. Tata is progressing on renewable electricity but has not yet reported renewable fuel use.
sections.targets: No Tata metric-specific renewable-fuel target identified. Jindal Stainless has relevant fuel-substitution initiatives, including biofuel replacing LSHS, and a net-zero target by 2050. SAIL’s targets focus on renewable capacity, decarbonisation technologies and green hydrogen pilots rather than reported renewable fuel consumption.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Zero may mean no renewable fuel consumed, no applicable use, or no reported renewable fuel in this category.
